title: Gathering motia
description: The process began with sourcing fresh Arabian jasmine from local gardens and South Asian florists. Each flower was handled with the same care my nano taught — picking them in the cool of early morning, before the sun could wilt the petals. The fragrance was overwhelming, filling the studio with a scent that made the work feel less like research and more like returning.

title: Pressing and preserving
description: Motia is ephemeral — it browns within hours of being picked. To preserve it, I developed a pressing technique using blotting paper and weighted frames, drying the flowers slowly over two weeks. Some were preserved in resin; others were left to age naturally, their browning becoming part of the material narrative.

title: Developing the textile concept
description: The collection grew from a simple observation: the motia flower is worn, not displayed. It sits in hair, on wrists, around necks. The textiles needed to carry the same intimacy — garments that touch the skin the way a garland does. I explored natural fabrics in the flower's colour range: raw silk, unbleached cotton, handwoven khadi.

title: Pattern making
description: Patterns were drafted by hand on brown paper — the same kind nano uses to line her shelves. Each garment began as a flat shape inspired by South Asian silhouettes: the kameez, the dupatta, the shalwar. These were then deconstructed and reassembled into contemporary forms that retained the original gesture.

title: Garland threading
description: Learning to thread motia garlands — gajra — was a form of embodied research. My grandmother's hands moved through the flowers with an ease that took me weeks to approach. The repetitive act of threading became meditative: a stitch-by-stitch conversation between my body and hers across time.

title: Final photoshoot preparation
description: The photoshoot required fresh motia — dozens of garlands sourced the morning of the shoot. The set was dressed to echo the domestic warmth of a South Asian home: linen drapes, copper vessels, warm light. The model was briefed to move slowly, gently — as though she were alone in her grandmother's house.
